figures.caption: Figure 1: Market vs. autarky. A Wealth Lorenz curves (Gini coefficient measures inequality; 0 = perfect equality, 1 = one agent holds everything; market = 0.33, autarky = 0.42). B Contract award Lorenz curves (market Gini = 0.39, autarky = 0.28). C Task quality distributions (market mean = 0.55, autarky = 0.46; d = +0.19, p < 0.001).1 Full comparison in Appendix D.1.

Table 1: Model families and per-million-token backbone prices ($). These prices govern the Trader’s strategic reasoning cost; task execution uses a shared Worker tier (Haiku/Sonnet/Opus), so the cost spread reflects heterogeneity in decision-making, not in execution.

Figure 6: Reputation predicts wealth. Agents who receive higher average payment ratios (better reputation) accumulate more wealth (r = 0.44–0.50, p < 0.001 across seeds). The relationship holds for both model families (a) and skill clusters (b), confirming that bilateral reputation is an effective, if noisy, information mechanism. Claude agents cluster at lower reputation and lower wealth; DeepSeek agents occupy the upper range.

Figure 7: False dispute rates: the fraction of objectively adequate work (q ≥ 0.5) that nonetheless receives dispute-level payment. (a) GLM posters have the highest false dispute rate (16.7%, ± SE across 3 seeds), consistent with their elevated punishment-theme scores in the embedding analysis. GPT posters are the most generous (2.0%). (b) Skill clusters show less variation than model families, suggesting that false disputes are driven more by poster identity than by task domain.

Figure 2: Emergent network structure (3-seed baseline; shading shows ± SE). A Role emergence: each marker is one agent (hollow = R6, filled = R24; size proportional to balance). Arrows show how agents drift from R6 to R24. Some families move right (become net contractors); others move left (net posters). B Volume Gini (blue, left axis) measures how unevenly trade volume is distributed; HHI (red) measures market concentration; trading pairs (green, right axis) counts unique buyer–seller connections. C Reciprocity (fraction of edges with a return edge) by family. All families far exceed the random baseline (∼8%).

Figure 3: Trade mechanics. A Reputation vs. final wealth by model family (r = 0.44, p < 0.001). B Bid price distribution by family (grey = all agents combined). C False dispute rate over 24 rounds (3-seed mean ± SD, with rolling average and trend).

Figure 8: Bid price distributions. (a) DeepSeek consistently underbids (median $2.5), while Claude and GPT bid highest (median $4–5), reflecting model-specific priors about fair prices. (b) Skill clusters show more uniform bidding, with coding tasks attracting slightly higher bids due to greater complexity. The grey “All” violin in the main-text version shows the pooled distribution for reference.

Figure 9: Final belief sentiment polarity (positive = optimistic, negative = pessimistic), computed via TextBlob on 850 agent belief states across 34 simulations. (a) DeepSeek beliefs are the most positive (mean = 0.27); GPT the least (0.16). GLM shows the widest variance. (b) Skill clusters are more uniform, suggesting that belief polarity is primarily a modelfamily property rather than a task-domain effect.

Figure 10: Profit and sentiment. (a) Mean contractor profit varies substantially by task domain: coding/engineering yields the lowest profit per transaction, while data-querying and web/media yield the highest, reflecting differences in task difficulty and bid competition. (b) Approve decisions carry positive sentiment (p̄ = +0.33) while disputes are weakly negative (p̄ = −0.12), with significant per-family differences (Mann–Whitney U, p < 0.05).
Table 3: Market API: ten read-only, cost-free queries available to all agents.

Figure 11: Skill-level payment analysis. (a) Payment ratio distributions by skill cluster. Web/media contractors receive the highest median payment ratio despite having the fewest transactions; coding/engineering has the widest spread. (b) Cross-skill payment heatmap (poster skill × contractor skill). Diagonal cells (skill-matched trades) tend to receive slightly higher payment, but the difference is not statistically significant (p = 0.51), suggesting that posters cannot reliably distinguish skill-matched from mismatched work at evaluation time.

Figure 4: Ablation effect sizes (Cohen’s d vs. baseline) for six institutional conditions across multiple metrics. Solid bars: p < 0.05; faded: not significant. Transparency produces the largest single effect: cross-family trade collapses (d = −1.76, p < 0.001). Fierce selection degrades all metrics simultaneously.

Figure 5: Agent personality and belief. A Theme fingerprint by model family: each bar shows how strongly a family’s evaluation reasoning aligns with eight semantic themes (trust, fairness, cooperation, reward, punishment, risk, strategic, exploitation), measured by embedding projection. B Final belief polarity by skill cluster: sentiment polarity (positive = optimistic, negative = pessimistic) of each agent’s final belief state. Additional breakdowns in Appendix D.

Table 4: Oracle poster model: conditional payment distribution from the three baseline seeds (1,957 transactions). Payment exhibits a sharp threshold at q = 0.5: failures (q < 0.5) receive ρ̄ ≈ 0.60–0.67 regardless of whether they are complete or partial, while adequate work (q ≥ 0.5) jumps to ρ̄ = 0.87–0.98.
Table 2: Configuration parameters and their economic mechanisms.

Figure 12: Extended network analysis (4 panels). A Role emergence: model families differentiate between net contractors (right) and net posters (left) from R6 to R24. Marker size proportional to total trade volume. B Three concentration metrics: Volume Gini (blue) rises from ∼30% to 40%; HHI (red) spikes early then stabilises; unique trading pairs (green) grow to 300+. C Reciprocity (fraction of edges with a return edge) by family. All families exceed the random baseline (∼8%); DeepSeek reaches ∼65%. D Division of labour at R24: contracts posted vs. won. Green zone = net contractors; red = net posters.

Figure 13: Wealth and reputation trajectories over 24 rounds (3-seed baseline, 1,957 transactions). Thin lines: individual agents; bold: group averages; dashed line: autarky mean balance. A Wealth by model family. B Wealth by skill cluster. C Reputation (cumulative mean ρ) by family. D Reputation by skill. Wealth trajectories diverge by round 6; reputation stabilises by round 15–20.

Table 5: Market vs. autarky comparison across seven metrics. The market dominates on six of seven dimensions; the sole exception is contract award concentration, reflecting competitive advantage of successful agents.
